---
title: "From Data to Dashboard: Penguins on the Map"
subtitle: "GISCI 343, Week 8"
author:
  - name: Hyesop Shin
    orcid: 0000-0003-2637-7933
    email: hyesop.shin@auckland.ac.nz
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Auckland
        address: 23 Symonds Street
        city: Auckland
        postal-code: 1010
date: 05/04/2026
date-format: long
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github
    transition: slide
    logo: Figs/uoa-logo.png
    css: Figs/logo.css
    code-block-height: 400px
    #smaller: true
    footer: "GISCI 343 | Week 8 | From Data to Dashboard"
jupyter: python3
---

## Today's plan

1. Quick recap of Weeks 6 and 7
2. The palmerpenguins dataset, with real coordinates
3. ipyleaflet: plotting penguins on a map
4. Wrapping the map in Shiny (notebook to app)
5. Popups and action buttons
6. **Group activity**: complete TASKs 1 to 5 in `app.py`
7. **Pair quiz** (submit on Canvas before you leave)

---

## Recap: the Shiny building blocks (Week 6)

Every Shiny app has the same three pieces:

```python
from shiny import App, ui, render

app_ui = ui.page_sidebar(...)   # 1. UI: what the user sees
def server(input, output, session):
    @render.plot                # 2. Server: how outputs respond
    def chart(): ...

app = App(app_ui, server)       # 3. App: connects them
```

**Reactivity** is the key idea: when an input changes, any output that reads it re-runs automatically. You describe _what depends on what_, and Shiny handles _when_.

---

## Recap: reactive nodes (Week 6) {.smaller}

| Decorator | Purpose | Cached? |
|-----------|---------|:-------:|
| `@reactive.calc` | Shared filtered data (call with `()`) | Yes |
| `@reactive.effect` | Side effects (logging, writing files) | No |
| `@render.plot` / `@render.text` | Sends results to the browser | No |
| `@reactive.event(input.x)` | Only runs when `input.x` fires | -- |

Rule of thumb: if two outputs need the same filtered data, lift it into a `@reactive.calc`.

---

## Recap: your planning work (Week 7)

Last week you wrote `plan.md` covering:

- **Motivation and audience**: what question does your dashboard answer?
- **Data and preparation**: which datasets, what cleaning steps, what limitations?
- **Technical architecture**: a narrative of what the user sees and does

You also ran a working ipyleaflet map inside Shiny using the NX2 bus route example.

. . .

Today we build on that. Instead of bus routes, we will use **penguins** to practise the full workflow: load data, add coordinates, map it, make it reactive.

---

## The palmerpenguins dataset {.smaller}

The `palmerpenguins` package provides measurements for 344 penguins from three species, collected at Palmer Station, Antarctica. We have prepared a version with individual latitude/longitude coordinates for each penguin (jittered around island centroids).

```{python}
import pandas as pd

penguins_geo = pd.read_csv("data/penguins_geo.csv")
print(f"{len(penguins_geo)} penguins, {penguins_geo['species'].nunique()} species, {penguins_geo['island'].nunique()} islands")
penguins_geo[["species", "island", "bill_length_mm", "body_mass_g", "latitude", "longitude"]].head(8)
```

---

## Three species, three islands

| Species | Island(s) | What to look for |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Adelie** | Torgersen, Biscoe, Dream | Found on all three islands |
| **Gentoo** | Biscoe only | Largest body mass |
| **Chinstrap** | Dream only | Longest bills relative to body size |

. . .

These islands are in the **Palmer Archipelago**, off the Antarctic Peninsula (roughly 64 to 65 degrees S). Each penguin in our dataset has its own coordinate, so they spread out across each island rather than stacking on a single point.

---

## How the coordinates were made

The original `palmerpenguins` package has no latitude/longitude. We created the dataset by:

1. Looking up approximate centroids for each island (Torgersen: -64.77, -64.08; Biscoe: -65.43, -65.50; Dream: -64.73, -64.23)
2. Jittering each penguin's position by a small random offset (~500m) to simulate individual nest sites

```python
# The idea behind the jittering:
base_lat, base_lon = island_coords[island]
lat = base_lat + np.random.uniform(-0.008, 0.008)
lon = base_lon + np.random.uniform(-0.012, 0.012)
```

For your Assignment 2, your data likely already has real coordinates, so you will not need this step.

---

## Your Turn: explore the data (Quiz 1-2)

With your partner, run the following in a notebook and discuss:

```python
import pandas as pd
penguins_geo = pd.read_csv("data/penguins_geo.csv")

# 1. How many penguins per island?
print(penguins_geo.groupby("island")["species"].count())

# 2. Which species appears on more than one island?
print(penguins_geo.groupby("species")["island"].nunique())

# 3. What is the mean body mass for each species?
print(penguins_geo.groupby("species")["body_mass_g"].mean())
```

Think about: which of these summaries could become an output in a dashboard?

---

## Our first penguin map: one marker per island
Run `popup_markers.py`


```{python}
from ipyleaflet import Map, Marker, basemaps

island_centres = penguins_geo.groupby("island").agg(
    lat=("latitude", "mean"), lon=("longitude", "mean")
).reset_index()

m = Map(center=(-64.8, -64.5), zoom=9, basemap=basemaps.Esri.WorldImagery)
for _, row in island_centres.iterrows():
    m.add(Marker(
        location=(row["lat"], row["lon"]),
        title=row["island"],
        draggable=False,
    ))
m
```

---

## Every penguin on the map

Since each penguin has its own coordinate, we can plot all 344 as individual `CircleMarker`s. Click any dot to see its island and species:

```{python}
from ipyleaflet import Map, CircleMarker, basemaps
from ipywidgets import HTML

m = Map(center=(-64.8, -64.5), zoom=9, basemap=basemaps.Esri.WorldImagery)

for _, row in penguins_geo.dropna(subset=["latitude"]).iterrows():
    marker = CircleMarker(
        location=(row["latitude"], row["longitude"]),
        radius=3,
        color="#447099",
        fill_color="#447099",
        fill_opacity=0.5,
    )
    marker.popup = HTML(value=f"<b>{row['island']}</b>: {row['species']}")
    m.add(marker)
m
```

Three geographic clusters appear, but some islands host more than one species. You cannot tell which is which yet because every dot is the same colour.

---

## Colouring by species

Each species gets its own colour, so we can see how they distribute across islands:

```{python}
from ipyleaflet import Map, CircleMarker, basemaps
from ipywidgets import HTML

species_colours = {"Adelie": "#ff6f00", "Gentoo": "#1565c0", "Chinstrap": "#2e7d32"}

m = Map(center=(-64.8, -64.5), zoom=9, basemap=basemaps.Esri.WorldImagery)

for _, row in penguins_geo.dropna(subset=["latitude"]).iterrows():
    colour = species_colours[row["species"]]
    marker = CircleMarker(
        location=(row["latitude"], row["longitude"]),
        radius=3,
        color=colour,
        fill_color=colour,
        fill_opacity=0.6,
    )
    marker.popup = HTML(
        value=f"<b>{row['species']}</b><br>Island: {row['island']}<br>Mass: {row['body_mass_g']} g"
    )
    m.add(marker)
m
```

Now you can see that islands are **not** single-species. Biscoe has both orange (Adelie) and blue (Gentoo). Dream has orange (Adelie) and green (Chinstrap). Only Torgersen is single-species (Adelie only). Click any dot to confirm.

## Your Turn: add species breakdown to popups

Modify the popup example so that each island marker shows a **breakdown by species**.

Run `popup_species_breakdown.py`

---

## From notebook to Shiny: the pattern

We have built the map step by step in a notebook. Now let us move it into a Shiny app. The pattern is always the same:

```
1. Get the code working in a notebook (static)
2. Identify the variable the user should control
3. Replace that variable with an input widget
4. Wrap the output in a render function
5. Done: Shiny handles the rest
```

. . .

For our penguin map, the variable the user should control is **species**.

---

## Step 1: static code (already done)

We already have this working:

```python
species = "Adelie"   # <-- the variable the user will control
sel = penguins_geo[penguins_geo["species"] == species]

m = Map(center=(-64.8, -64.5), zoom=9, basemap=basemaps.Esri.WorldImagery)
# ... add markers for sel ...
```

---

## Step 2: add an input widget

```python
app_ui = ui.page_sidebar(
    ui.sidebar(
        ui.input_radio_buttons(
            "species", "Species",
            choices=["Adelie", "Gentoo", "Chinstrap"],
        ),
    ),
    output_widget("map"),
    title="Palmer Penguins Explorer",
)
```

The `id` is `"species"`, so in the server we read it with `input.species()`.

---

## Step 3: wrap in a render function

```python
def server(input, output, session):
    @render_widget
    def map():
        species = input.species()                          # <-- reactive
        sel = penguins_geo[penguins_geo["species"] == species]

        m = Map(center=(-64.8, -64.5), zoom=9,
                basemap=basemaps.Esri.WorldImagery)
        for _, row in sel.dropna(subset=["latitude"]).iterrows():
            marker = CircleMarker(
                location=(row["latitude"], row["longitude"]),
                radius=4, color="#447099",
                fill_color="#447099", fill_opacity=0.7,
            )
            marker.popup = HTML(value=f"<b>{row['species']}</b><br>{row['island']}")
            m.add(marker)
        return m
```

Every time the user picks a different species, `map()` re-runs and the browser updates.

---

## The complete app

```python
from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
from shinywidgets import output_widget, render_widget
from ipyleaflet import Map, CircleMarker, basemaps
from ipywidgets import HTML
import pandas as pd

penguins_geo = pd.read_csv("data/penguins_geo.csv")

app_ui = ui.page_sidebar(
    ui.sidebar(
        ui.input_checkbox_group(
            "species", "Species",
            choices=["Adelie", "Gentoo", "Chinstrap"],
            selected=["Adelie", "Gentoo", "Chinstrap"],
        ),
    ),
    ui.output_text("summary"),
    output_widget("map"),
    title="Palmer Penguins Explorer",
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @reactive.calc
    def filtered():
        return penguins_geo[penguins_geo["species"].isin(input.species())]

    @render_widget
    def map():
        sel = filtered().dropna(subset=["latitude"])
        m = Map(center=(-64.8, -64.5), zoom=9,
                basemap=basemaps.Esri.WorldImagery)
        for _, row in sel.iterrows():
            colour = species_colours[row["species"]]
            marker = CircleMarker(
                location=(row["latitude"], row["longitude"]),
                radius=4, color=colour,
                fill_color=colour, fill_opacity=0.7,
            )
            marker.popup = HTML(
                value=f"<b>{row['species']}</b><br>Island: {row['island']}<br>Mass: {row['body_mass_g']} g"
            )
            m.add(marker)
        return m

    @render.text
    def summary():
        sel = filtered()
        n = len(sel.dropna(subset=["bill_length_mm"]))
        avg_mass = sel["body_mass_g"].mean()
        species_list = ", ".join(sorted(sel["species"].unique()))
        return (f"{species_list}: {n} penguins, "
                f"avg body mass {avg_mass:,.0f} g")

app = App(app_ui, server)
```

---

## Notice the `@reactive.calc`

```python
@reactive.calc
def filtered():
    return penguins_geo[penguins_geo["species"].isin(input.species())]
```

Both `map()` and `summary()` call `filtered()`. The filtering runs **once** per input change, not twice.

This is the same pattern from Week 6, and the same pattern you should use in Assignment 2.

---

## Your Turn: run the complete app

Copy the complete app code into an `app.py` file and run it with `shiny run --reload app.py`.

1. Does the map update when you click a different species?
2. Does the summary text update at the same time?
3. Try adding `print(f"filtered ran for {input.species()}")` inside `filtered()`. How many times does it print when you switch species?


## Adding a second input: island filter

What if the user also wants to filter by island? Add another widget and extend the calc:

```python
# In app_ui, inside ui.sidebar():
ui.input_checkbox_group(
    "islands", "Islands",
    choices=["Torgersen", "Biscoe", "Dream"],
    selected=["Torgersen", "Biscoe", "Dream"],
),

# In server:
@reactive.calc
def filtered():
    df = penguins_geo[penguins_geo["species"].isin(input.species())]
    return df[df["island"].isin(input.islands())]
```

Every output that calls `filtered()` will update when _either_ input changes.

---

## Adding a chart alongside the map

```python
from shiny import render

# In app_ui, add after output_widget("map"):
ui.card(
    ui.card_header("Bill length distribution"),
    ui.output_plot("hist"),
),

# In server:
@render.plot
def hist():
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    sel = filtered()
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
    ax.hist(sel["bill_length_mm"].dropna(), bins=15,
            color="#447099", edgecolor="white")
    ax.set_xlabel("Bill length (mm)")
    ax.set_ylabel("Count")
    return fig
```

Map and chart share `filtered()`. One `@reactive.calc`, two outputs.

---

## The full dashboard layout

```python
app_ui = ui.page_sidebar(
    ui.sidebar(
        ui.input_checkbox_group("species", "Species",
                                choices=["Adelie", "Gentoo", "Chinstrap"],
                                selected=["Adelie", "Gentoo", "Chinstrap"]),
        ui.input_checkbox_group("islands", "Islands",
                                choices=["Torgersen", "Biscoe", "Dream"],
                                selected=["Torgersen", "Biscoe", "Dream"]),
        width=280,
    ),
    ui.output_text("summary"),
    ui.layout_columns(
        ui.card(ui.card_header("Map"), output_widget("map")),
        ui.card(ui.card_header("Bill lengths"), ui.output_plot("hist")),
        col_widths=[7, 5],
    ),
    title="Palmer Penguins Explorer",
)
```

---

## Two reminders for ipyleaflet

1. **Always `.to_crs(4326)`** when loading a GeoDataFrame. ipyleaflet expects WGS84. If your data is in NZTM (EPSG:2193), the map will appear empty with no error.

2. **Return a new `Map()` inside `@render_widget`** every time. Do not create the map at module level and try to mutate it.

```python
# Wrong:
m = Map(...)           # created once, never refreshed
@render_widget
def map():
    m.add_layer(...)   # tries to mutate, will not update
    return m

# Right:
@render_widget
def map():
    m = Map(...)       # new map each time
    m.add_layer(...)
    return m
```

---


## Group activity: complete TASKs 1 to 5 {.smaller}

Open the `app.py` file provided in the `week08/` folder. It is a working skeleton of the Palmer Penguins Explorer. Your job is to complete **five small tasks** (each is only 1 to 2 lines of code):

| Task | What to do | Where |
|------|-----------|-------|
| TASK 1 | Add island filtering to `filtered()` | `df = df[df["island"].isin(input.islands())]` |
| TASK 2 | Colour each dot by species | `colour = species_colours[row["species"]]` |
| TASK 3 | Uncomment the popup lines | Already written, just remove `#` |
| TASK 4 | Add island names to the summary text | Build `islands` string, add to return |
| TASK 5 | Colour each histogram bar by species | `color=species_colours[sp]` |

Run with: `shiny run --reload app.py`

---

## Tips for the group activity

- The app **already runs** before you change anything. Each task makes it better.
- Look at the **existing pattern** above each task. For example, species filtering is done for you; island filtering follows the same `.isin()` pattern.
- After each task, save the file. Because you used `--reload`, the browser updates automatically.
- If you get stuck, compare with the code we built together in the earlier slides.


## Canvas quiz 3

Your GeoDataFrame is in EPSG:2193 (NZTM). You load it and add it to an ipyleaflet map, but the map appears empty. What is the most likely cause?

A. The file is corrupt
B. ipyleaflet expects EPSG:4326 (WGS84), and you forgot `.to_crs(4326)`
C. You need to install a separate projection library
D. NZTM coordinates are too large for ipyleaflet to render

---

## Checklist before you leave

- [ ] Completed TASKs 1 to 5 in `app.py`
- [ ] Pair quiz submitted on Canvas
- [ ] Understand the notebook-to-Shiny pattern
- [ ] Know how `@reactive.calc` shares data between outputs



## From penguins to your Assignment 2

The penguin app is a toy example, but the structure maps directly to your project:

| Penguin app | Your dashboard |
|-------------|---------------|
| `penguins_geo.csv` | Your dataset (AT, Census, crashes) |
| `input.species()` | Your filter (route, year, suburb) |
| `@reactive.calc filtered()` | Your shared filtered dataset |
| `CircleMarker` | Your map layer (points or polygons) |
| `@render.plot hist` | Your chart (bar, line, histogram) |
| `@reactive.event` | Optional: "Apply" button if filtering is slow |

---

## Further reading

- [palmerpenguins for Python](https://github.com/mcnakhaee/palmerpenguins)
- [ipyleaflet documentation](https://ipyleaflet.readthedocs.io/)
- [Shiny for Python: Components gallery](https://shiny.posit.co/py/components/)
- [Shiny for Python: Reactive patterns](https://shiny.posit.co/py/docs/reactive-patterns.html)
- Textbook 3.5 (Mapping Auckland Open Data) for `GeoData` patterns
- [Python Shiny gallery](https://shiny.posit.co/py/gallery/) for full working examples
